blocks.content: By the time they got on their bikes the storm's leading edge was moving faster than it had any right to, and the first fat drops had started hitting the pavement — scattered, one every few feet, like the sky was deciding before it committed.

It came down the way Southern summer storms do — not gradually but all at once, as if someone opened a valve. The light went from amber to bruised violet in thirty seconds, and the gutters ran red clay water almost immediately.
They were soaked before they'd made it one block.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: It wasn't supposed to come this fast!

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You were not right last time!
Eli was whooping ahead of them. Savannah had pulled her shirt up over her head, which accomplished very little except giving her somewhere to direct her dignity.

They ducked under the overhang of the hardware store on Cedar Street, all four bikes clattering against the wall, everyone breathing hard from the sprint. The rain hammered the street — the sound of it on the metal awning almost too loud to talk over.
Eli shook his head like a wet dog. Water went in every direction.
They stood there, dripping, watching the street disappear behind a gray curtain of rain.
Daisy was already looking at Kit.
